did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a deep learning model with ConvNeXt as the backbone for the multi-class classification of skin diseases (Acne, Actinic Keratosis, and Benign Tumors). The methodology involves fine-tuning the ConvNeXt architecture on a dataset sourced from Kaggle to achieve diagnostic classification results.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental results from a deep learning study using the ConvNeXt architecture for multi-class classification of skin diseases. It details a specific methodology including data preprocessing, model fine-tuning on a Kaggle dataset, and evaluation metrics (91% accuracy, precision, recall, F1-scores). Although published in a conference proceedings (DASA), it reports new empirical findings rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: ConvNeXt-Powered Framework for Accurate Multi-Class Classification of Skin Diseases in Clinical Screening
journal: 2025 International Conference on Decision Aid Sciences and Applications (DASA)
brief_summary: This study introduces a deep learning framework using ConvNeXt for the multi-class classification of three skin diseases: Acne, Actinic Keratosis, and Benign Tumors. The model was trained and evaluated on a dataset sourced from Kaggle, which was preprocessed with resizing, normalization, and denoising. The data was split into training, validation, and test sets in a 70:15:15 ratio, with the ConvNeXt architecture fine-tuned for classification. The proposed model achieved an overall accuracy of 91%, along with high precision, recall, and F1-scores across all classes. Confusion matrix analysis indicated robust performance, particularly in distinguishing Acne and Actinic Keratosis, despite some visual overlaps with Benign Tumors. The findings support ConvNeXt's suitability as a clinical decision support tool for accurate and efficient dermatological screening.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the three classes in the multi-class classification task. The dataset contained 593 training images and 65 test images for this class. The model achieved a precision of 0.83 and recall of 0.95.

One of the three classes in the multi-class classification task. The dataset contained 748 training images and 83 test images for this class. The model achieved a precision of 0.93 and recall of 0.92. The paper notes it can be confused with pre-cancerous lesions.

One of the three classes in the multi-class classification task. The dataset contained 1093 training images and 121 test images for this class. The model achieved a precision of 0.95 and recall of 0.88.

all_task_types.target: Multi-class skin disease diagnosis (Acne, Actinic Keratosis, Benign Tumors)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs multi-class classification of three specific skin diseases using a ConvNeXt model. The task involves predicting discrete diagnostic categories (Acne, Actinic Keratosis, Benign Tumors) from skin lesion images. The model was trained with categorical cross-entropy loss, and performance was evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, F1-scores, and a confusion matrix. The dataset consisted of 2,703 images split into training (70%), validation (15%), and test (15%) sets.

datasets_used.name: Kaggle Skin Disease Dataset
datasets_used.description: A three-class skin-lesion dataset sourced from Kaggle [24], consisting of colored photographs with varying resolutions and illumination conditions representing real-world clinical variability.

use.training: Used for fine-tuning the ConvNeXt model (2,191 images).
use.validation: Used for model selection and early stopping (243 images, 10% of the original training set).
use.testing: Used for final evaluation of accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-scores (269 images).

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate class-wise prediction performance, identify misclassifications, and assess the model's ability to discriminate between skin disease classes (Acne, Actinic Keratosis, Benign Tumors).
To provide overall summary metrics for precision, recall, and F1-score, confirming the generalizability and balanced performance of the model across classes.
To monitor model performance during training and ensure class distribution consistency for model selection and early stopping.
To assess optimization convergence, regularization effectiveness, and generalization performance, identifying overfitting or underfitting trends.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, F1-scores, and confusion matrix analysis.

Acne.confusion_details: 62 correctly classified; 1 misclassified as Actinic Keratosis; 2 misclassified as Benign Tumor

Actinic Keratosis.confusion_details: 76 correctly classified; 3 misclassified as Acne; 4 misclassified as Benign Tumors

Benign Tumors.confusion_details: 106 correctly classified; 10 misclassified as Acne; 5 misclassified as Actinic Keratosis

Kaggle Skin Disease Dataset.notes: The model achieved an overall accuracy of 91%. Confusion matrix analysis indicated robust performance, particularly in distinguishing Acne and Actinic Keratosis, despite some visual overlaps with Benign Tumors. The highest precision was observed for Benign Tumors (0.95), while Acne had the highest recall (0.95).

architecture_details.backbone_type: Modernized convolutional network inspired by Vision Transformers (ViTs)
architecture_details.stages: Four consecutive ConvNeXt stages with increasing depth and channel dimensions (96, 192, 384, and 768 channels)

classification_or_output_head.type: Fully connected layer with Softmax activation

preprocessing_techniques: Resizing images to 224x224 pixels to match ConvNeXt input size
Normalization of pixel intensity values to [0, 1]
Contrast Limited Adaptive Histogram Equalization (CLAHE) to enhance local contrast and visualize skin lesions
Gaussian filtering/denoising to reduce sensor noise and high-frequency artifacts
Normalization to match the mean and standard deviation of ImageNet for transfer learning compatibility
conclusions: The ConvNeXt architecture is effective for the automated multi-class classification of Acne, Actinic Keratosis, and Benign Tumors, achieving an overall accuracy of 91% with balanced precision, recall, and F1-scores.
The model demonstrates robust generalization and discriminative ability, particularly in distinguishing Acne and Actinic Keratosis, despite visual overlaps with Benign Tumors.
The study supports the suitability of ConvNeXt as a reliable clinical decision-support tool for early and accurate dermatological diagnosis.
A key limitation is the use of a comparatively limited dataset, which may restrict generalizability to heterogeneous clinical settings.

Future work will focus on extending the dataset to include broader disease categories, uncommon disorders, and diverse demographics, as well as integrating multimodal data (e.g., patient history, dermoscopic images) and explainable AI methods.
Deployment on edge devices or mobile apps is proposed to enable real-time, low-cost screening in both urban clinics and resource-constrained environments.
important_remarks: The study relies on a single, publicly available Kaggle dataset (2,703 images) without external validation, limiting the assessment of generalizability to heterogeneous clinical populations.
The dataset exhibits class imbalance, with Benign Tumors comprising 44.9% of the training set compared to 24.4% for Acne, which may bias the model toward the majority class.
The paper does not report any statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values or confidence intervals) for the reported performance metrics (91% accuracy).

Visual overlaps between classes (e.g., redness, scaling) lead to misclassifications, particularly between Benign Tumors and other classes, highlighting limitations in feature discriminability for visually similar lesions.

The model was trained on colored photographs with varying resolutions and illumination conditions, introducing potential variability in input quality that was not explicitly controlled for beyond standard preprocessing.

No data leakage risks were explicitly addressed, though the use of a stratified validation set for early stopping suggests some data leakage potential if the validation set was not strictly held out from the training process.

The study focuses on only three skin disease classes, limiting its clinical relevance for broader dermatological screening applications that require multi-class differentiation of numerous conditions.

The paper acknowledges the need for future work to include multimodal patient data and diverse demographics to improve real-world applicability, indicating current limitations in demographic representation.
